evidence: pom.xml has only 6 production runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, jackson-databind, slf4j-api, logback-classic, log4j-to-slf4j — a lean and purposeful set

jackson-databind explicitly versioned at ${jackson.version}=2.17.2 (line 54-56 pom.xml) while jackson-bom at the same version is imported via dependencyManagement — the explicit version is redundant but not conflicting

BlackLab 4.0.0 is not on Maven Central; standard `mvn clean package` fails without first running ./install-blacklab.sh which downloads from GitHub releases; a raw-GitHub-URL fallback profile exists but carries no SLA
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose (single JSON processor: Jackson; single logging facade: SLF4J with logback-classic as runtime provider)
log4j-to-slf4j bridge correctly routes BlackLab's log4j calls through the project's SLF4J stack without pulling a full log4j runtime
jspecify annotations declared with scope=provided — zero runtime footprint, no JAR bloat
All test dependencies (JUnit 5 jupiter-api, jupiter-engine, platform-launcher) correctly scoped as test
CLAUDE.md documentation still lists 'Fastjson 2.0.25' as a dependency but fastjson is not present in pom.xml; Jackson is the actual JSON library — stale doc, no actual duplicate dependency

dependency_health.dimension_character: The dependency footprint is impressively lean for a corpus-analysis tool: six production runtime dependencies, no duplicate libraries, no heavy pull-ins for trivial use, and all test/annotation deps correctly scoped. The dominant health problem is the BlackLab 4.x distribution gap — the library is not on Maven Central, so every developer and CI pipeline must execute a manual pre-build install script or rely on a raw GitHub URL with no SLA. This single issue creates meaningful onboarding friction and CI brittleness that overshadows an otherwise clean picture.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The codebase sits well above a 'significant drag' (60) baseline on most dependency-health axes — lean set, no duplicates, no over-engineering — but the BlackLab distribution issue is a systemic, not cosmetic, problem: standard Maven toolchain fails without a manual bootstrap step, which is the kind of issue the dimension specifically targets. The redundant jackson-databind version pin is minor (no conflict) but is a known open item. Together these pull the score into the low-to-mid 60s rather than the solid 70-80 range a well-resolved dependency graph would earn.

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.x absent from Maven Central forces a manual pre-build install step and a fragile raw-GitHub-URL fallback
evidence: pom.xml comment (line 113-130): 'BlackLab 4.x is NOT published to Maven Central … REQUIRED FIRST STEP: run ./install-blacklab.sh'
A `blacklab-github-fallback` Maven profile exists that points to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/… with checksumPolicy=warn — no SLA, can break on rate-limit, branch deletion, or repo rename
install-blacklab.sh downloads from GitHub releases and runs `mvn install:install-file` into the local ~/.m2 repo; any CI pipeline without this pre-step fails at dependency resolution
issues.suggestion: Mirror both blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser JARs to a stable private registry (GitHub Packages, Nexus, or Artifactory) and reference that registry in pom.xml. This eliminates the manual script dependency and the unreliable raw-GitHub URL, making standard `mvn clean package` work out of the box for all developers and CI runners.

issues.summary: jackson-databind carries an explicit version tag that duplicates what jackson-bom already manages
evidence: pom.xml dependencyManagement imports jackson-bom at 2.17.2 (lines 27-32), which already pins jackson-databind to 2.17.2
pom.xml dependencies block also declares jackson-databind with <version>${jackson.version}</version> = 2.17.2 (lines 54-56)
Versions currently agree, so there is no runtime conflict, but the redundant pin means a future BOM upgrade will silently be shadowed by the explicit version unless both are updated in sync
issues.suggestion: Remove the <version> tag from the jackson-databind dependency declaration in pom.xml; let the imported jackson-bom manage the version. This eliminates the dual-maintenance risk and is the standard pattern when a BOM is already in use.

root_causes: BlackLab 4.x has never been published to Maven Central by its maintainer — this is an upstream project decision, not a local one; the workaround (install script + fallback profile) is reasonable but inherently fragile
The jackson-bom was added after the jackson-databind version was already pinned; the explicit version was never cleaned up

add.description: Only six production runtime JARs are declared: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, jackson-databind, slf4j-api, logback-classic, and log4j-to-slf4j. No duplicate libraries, no heavy pull-ins for marginal use, and every dep is actively used in production code.
BlackLab brings a log4j dependency; the project bridges it through SLF4J via log4j-to-slf4j without pulling a full log4j runtime. This keeps the logging stack unified and avoids classpath conflicts.
org.jspecify:jspecify is declared with scope=provided, so nullability annotations are checked at compile time but contribute zero bytes to the shaded JAR at runtime.

CLAUDE.md documents 'Fastjson 2.0.25' as a project dependency, but fastjson is not present in pom.xml; Jackson (jackson-databind) is the actual JSON library. The documentation is stale but there is no actual duplicate JSON library in the build.
